coredata.dc:description: As the sixth leading cause of cancer death, esophageal cancer is threatening the life of people worldwide. Traditional treatments, such as surgery, chemotherapy, radiotherapy, are facing always augmented challenges including invasion, multidrug resistance (MDR), off-target toxicity. Chemo & Photodynamic synergistic therapy represents one promising strategy for improved treatment efficiency. But it is still hindered by the lack of tumor targeting, deleterious side effects, and unfavorable microenvironment for photodynamic therapy (PDT). To overcome those obstacles, one theranostic nano-assambly drug, GCDs-Ce6/Pt-EGF, was designed and fabricated. Green fluorescence carbon dots (GCDs) with the excellent optical properties, modifiability and low toxicity were prepared as drug carrier. Epidermal growth factor (EGF) was conjugated to the nano-assembly to realize tumor specific targeting. Chlorin e6 (Ce6) in the presence of laser irradiation achieved PDT by generating proapoptosis reactive oxygen species (ROS). Moreover, Ce6 incorporated into GCDs endowed the nano-assambly imaging ability and facilitate image-guided therapy. Pt(IV), cisplatin prodrug, in the nano-assambly depleted the glutathione (GSH) of tumor microenvironment when it was reduced to cytotoxicity Pt(II). Compared with single treatment, GCDs-Ce6/Pt-EGF exhibited enhanced tumor cell killing capacity and better biosafety in vitro and in vivo, especially for EGFR bearing tumor. It paved ways for developing novel theranostic agent to be potentially applied in clinic.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterization of GCDs. (A) TEM, and size distribution, (B) AFM images, (C) FTIR spectra, (D-G) XPS spectra and high resolution C1s, N1s and O1s, (H) Absorbance and emission spectra of GCDs (excitation at 430 nm).

Characterization of GCDs-Ce6/Pt-EGF. (A) TEM image (B) DLS size distribution of nano-assembly, (C) XPS spectra of Pt4f, (D) Zeta potential values (measured in deionized water at pH = 7.4) of nano-assembly. (E) UV-Vis spectra of different units. (F) Cisplatin release from GCDs-Ce6/Pt-EGF under different physiological conditions (pH7.4, pH 6.5 and 10 mM GSH) at 37 ℃. (G-H) ROS production capacity of GCDs-Ce6/Pt-EGF detected by DCFH-DA probe (20 µM) and DPBF probe (60 µM) subject to 660 nm (0.5 W/cm2) irradiation for 0–5 min.

Cellular uptaken of GCDs-Ce6/Pt-EGF by KYSE-150. (A) Confocal fluorescence images of KYSE-150 cells incubated with GCDs-Ce6/Pt-EGF for different time. (Green fluorescence: GCDs, red fluorescence: Ce6). (B) Fluorescence intensity quantification of green channel (GCDs) and red channel (Ce6), (C) Flow cytometry analysis of KYSE-150 cells incubated with GCDs-Ce6/Pt-EGF for different time (Scale bar = 50 µm).

EGF mediated targeting activity of GCDs-Ce6/Pt-EGF. (A) Confocal fluorescence images of KYSE-150 cells incubated with GCDs (60 μg/mL), Ce6 (5.0 μg/mL), GCDs-Ce6-EGF (Ce6 5.0 μg/mL), and GCDs-Ce6/Pt-EGF (Ce6 5.0 μg/mL) for 4 h. (B) Quantitative analysis of KYSE-150 cells incubated with GCDs or Ce6 and GCDs-Ce6/Pt-EGF. (C) Confocal fluorescence images, (D) Fluorescence intensity quantification of KYSE-150 cells incubated with GCDs-Ce6/Pt-EGF with or without EGF pretreatment. (Scale bar = 50 µm). ( n = 3, P values for comparisons among groups by t-test were indicated by asterisks: *P＜0.05, **P＜0.01 and ***P＜0.001.).

Mechanism evaluation of cellular uptake. (A) Immunofluorescence results of EGFR expression in HEEC, KYSE-150 and MCF-7 cells. (Scale bar = 50 µm). (B) Quantitative analysis of result of Immunofluorescence results of EGFR expression. (C) CLSM images of HEEC, KYSE-150, MCF-7 cells incubated with GCDs-Ce6/Pt-EGF (Ce6 5.0 μg/mL) for 4 h. (scale bar = 50 µm). (D) Quantitative analysis of fluorescent signal of FITC in HEEC, KYSE-150, MCF-7. (n = 3, P values for comparisons among groups by t-test were indicated by asterisks: *P＜0.05, **P＜0.01 and ***P＜0.001.).

In vitro cell killing capacity drugs. (A) Cell viability of KYSE-150 cells incubated with GCDs, Ce6, GCDs-Ce6-EGF, Cisplatin and GCDs-Ce6/Pt-EGF for 24 h with or without 660 nm laser irradiation (0.5 W/cm2, 5 min). (B) Annexin V-FITC/PI co-staining flow cytometry of KYSE-150 cells incubated with GCDs (60 μg/mL), Ce6 (5.0 μg/mL), Cisplatin (11.35 μg/mL), GCDs-Ce6-EGF (Ce6 5.0 μg/mL) and GCDs-Ce6/Pt-EGF (Ce6 5.0 μg/mL) for 24 h with 660 nm laser irradiation (0.5 W/cm2, 5 min). (C) Images of KYSE-150 cells stained with calcein AM (green, live cells) and propidium iodide (red, dead cells) after different treatments. (Scale bar = 200 µm).

In vivo imaging and anti-tumor effects. (A) In vivo fluorescence images according to predesigned time points after intravenous injection of GCDs-Ce6/Pt-EGF (Ce6 2.0 mg/kg per mouse). (B) Ex vivo fluorescence distribution in tumor and major organs after intravenous injection of GCDs-Ce6/Pt-EGF (Ce6 2.0 mg/kg per mouse) at 24 h. (C) Treatment diagram of the experiment. (D) Changes in tumor appearance of tumor-bearing mice in various treatment groups. (Day 21, the position of the yellow circle was the location of the tumor). (E) The growth curve of tumors from nine groups after different treatments. (G-F) Tumor weight, and Changes in body weight in each group during treatment. (H) Ki67 staining and TUNEL staining of tumor tissues with different groups. (Scale bar = 200 µm). (1) PBS + L; (2) Ce6; (3) GCDs-Ce6-EGF; (4) Cisplatin; (5) Cisplatin + L; (6) GCDs-Ce6/Pt-EGF; (7) Ce6 + L; (8) GCDs-Ce6-EGF + L; (9) GCDs-Ce6/Pt-EGF + L. (n = 5, P values for comparisons among groups by t-test were indicated by asterisks: *P＜0.05, * *P＜0.01 and * **P＜0.001.).

Biosafety assessment. (A) Serum biochemical indices (ALT, AST and BUN) of tumor-bearing mice after i.v. injection in different treatment groups for 21 days. (B) H&E staining sections of the main organs (heart, liver, spleen, lung, and kidney) at 21 days. (Scale bar: 200 µm) (1) PBS + L; (2) Ce6; (3) GCDs-Ce6-EGF; (4) Cisplatin; (5) Cisplatin + L; (6) GCDs-Ce6/Pt-EGF; (7) Ce6 + L; (8) GCDs-Ce6-EGF + L; (9) GCDs-Ce6/Pt-EGF + L.
Schematic illustration of the synthesis, and working mechanism of the all-in-one theranostic nanoplatform, GCDs-Ce6/Pt-EGF, for enhanced chemo/photodynamic cancer therapy.

head.ce:title: Targeted chemo-photodynamic therapy toward esophageal cancer by GSH-sensitive theranostic nanoplatform

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: As the sixth leading cause of cancer death, esophageal cancer is threatening the life of people worldwide. Traditional treatments, such as surgery, chemotherapy, radiotherapy, are facing always augmented challenges including invasion, multidrug resistance (MDR), off-target toxicity. Chemo & Photodynamic synergistic therapy represents one promising strategy for improved treatment efficiency. But it is still hindered by the lack of tumor targeting, deleterious side effects, and unfavorable microenvironment for photodynamic therapy (PDT). To overcome those obstacles, one theranostic nano-assambly drug, GCDs-Ce6/Pt-EGF, was designed and fabricated. Green fluorescence carbon dots (GCDs) with the excellent optical properties, modifiability and low toxicity were prepared as drug carrier. Epidermal growth factor (EGF) was conjugated to the nano-assembly to realize tumor specific targeting. Chlorin e6 (Ce6) in the presence of laser irradiation achieved PDT by generating proapoptosis reactive oxygen species (ROS). Moreover, Ce6 incorporated into GCDs endowed the nano-assambly imaging ability and facilitate image-guided therapy. Pt(IV), cisplatin prodrug, in the nano-assambly depleted the glutathione (GSH) of tumor microenvironment when it was reduced to cytotoxicity Pt(II). Compared with single treatment, GCDs-Ce6/Pt-EGF exhibited enhanced tumor cell killing capacity and better biosafety in vitro and in vivo, especially for EGFR bearing tumor. It paved ways for developing novel theranostic agent to be potentially applied in clinic.

ce:section.ce:para: Esophageal cancer, the sixth leading cause of cancer death worldwide, is a major threat to human life [1]. In China, esophageal cancer is even more prevalent, which is listed the fourth cause of death of all malignant tumors [2]. Traditional treatments for esophageal cancer include surgery, radiotherapy, chemotherapy, and combinated therapy. As known, esophageal cancer is liable to metastasis, so surgery hardly eliminates the tumor completely [3]. Radiotherapy is also used for esophageal treatment, but the dose window of radiotherapy is narrow. Low-dose radionuclides have limited tumor cells killing effects, and high-dose radionuclides can cause normal cells death [4,5]. Chemotherapy via Cisplatin or Doxorubicin administration can kill the tumor cells even with metastasis and displays outstanding anti-tumor effect [6]. But chemotherapy often leads to severe liver and kidney injury, and long-term medication inevitable results in drug-resistant [7,8]. Combination therapy, such as multidrug combination [9,10], chemoradiotherapy [11], and chemoradiation with surgery [12], has improved treatment effect at a certain extent towards esophageal cancer in clinical. But they show no improvement in overall survival, a high in-hospital mortality rate and inherent anxiety. In addition, some promising therapies, such as photothermal therapy and oxidative therapy, have also been introduced to treat esophageal cancer [13]. However, these strategies are all prone to tumor recurrence and may rapidly kill healthy cells. Photodynamic therapy (PDT) via photosensitizer such as Chlorin e6 (Ce6) is a non-invasive tumor treatment strategy emerged in recent years, and it has been widely applied in breast cancer [14–16], liver cancer [17], prostate cancer [18], lung cancer [19], and esophageal cancer [20,21], and exhibits better biosafety and excellent therapeutic effects. However, hypoxia and excessive GSH in tumor microenvironment compromise the effect of PDT. So far outcome of esophageal cancer treatment is not satisfactory. The 5-year survival rates remain very poor (~10 %) [22]. There is an urgent need to develop a novel treatment strategy to enhance the therapeutic effect on esophageal cancer.

Nanoparticles (NPs) are the substances of which sizes are between about 1–100 nm and show properties that are not found in bulk samples of the same material. NPs have been utilized in broad fields such as drug fabrication and delivery [23–25]. Carbon nanodots (CDs) are new type of carbon NPs with uniformly dispersed sphere morphology [26]. CDs possess some properties including good light stability, low toxicity, and easy surface functionalization, which enable their application in fluorescence sensing [27,28], bioimaging [29–32] and drug delivery [33,34].

Tumor targeted drug delivery is one strategy to achieve tumor precise treatment. Modification of drugs could facilitate its recognition of tumor cells. The receptors expressed in the tumor cell surface were liable to be the candidate for drug targeting [35]. Among cell surface receptors, epidermal growth factor receptor (EGFR) was found to overexpressed in many types of cancers including esophageal cancer. EGFR in esophageal cancer cells was 20 times higher than that of normal esophageal mucosa [36]. The overexpression of EGFR protein was observed in 68 % of esophageal squamous cell carcinoma (ESCC) patients. Moreover, expression of EGFR was also closely related to clinical stage, tumor invasion and poor survival rate of esophageal cancer [37–40]. Hence, EGFR could be served as the target for antitumor drug delivery.

Herein, to overcome the defects of traditional tumor therapeutics, we have developed the all-in-one theranostic nanoplatform, GCDs-Ce6/Pt-EGF, toward esophageal cancer, as showed in Scheme 1 , green fluorescence carbon dots (GCDs) were firstly synthesized using p-aminobenzamide and p-aminosalicylic acid as raw materials through a one-step hydrothermal method. Subsequently the photosensitizer Ce6, the targeting unit epidermal growth factor (EGF), and chemotherapy agent Pt(IV) were grafted on GCDs to construct the nanoplatform, GCDs-Ce6/Pt-EGF, and it displayed excellent biosafety, tumor cell targeting and killing ability. Mechanistically, GCDs-Ce6/Pt-EGF targeted to esophageal cancer cell bearing EGFR. After internalized by tumor cells, Pt(IV) was reduced to Pt(II) by intracellular glutathione (GSH) and the latter killed the tumor cells [41,42]. Meanwhile, consumption of GSH by Pt(IV) enhanced the oxidative stress of tumor microenvironment [43,44]. Moreover, with the irradiation of wavelength 660 nm light, Ce6 as the photosensitizer generated reactive oxygen species (ROS) such as 1O2 and induced the tumor cell apoptosis. Both in vitro and in vivo results indicated that GCDs-Ce6/Pt-EGF achieved better esophageal tumor imaging and therapeutic effect. It thus paved ways for developing novel strategy of cancer theranostics.

P-aminosalicylic acid, p-aminobenzamide, Chlorin e6, Rhodamine 6 G, 1-ethyl-3-(3-dimethylaminopropyl) carbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC·HCl), O-phenylenediamine, 2,7-Dichlorodihydrofluorescein diacetate (DCFH-DA) and propidium iodide (PI) were obtained from Aladdin Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Naphthalene-2,3-dicarboxaldehyde (NDA) was purchased from MedChemExpress (Beijing, China). 3-(4,5-Dimethylthiazol-2-yl)− 2,5-diph-enyltetrazolium bromide (MTT), N-Hydroxysuccinimide (NHS), 1,3-Diphenylisobenzofu-ran (DPBF), DMEM medium, 1640 medium, fetal bovine serum, trypsin and calcein acetoxymethyl ester were purchased from Solarbio (Beijing, China). Annexin V-FITC and recombinant human EGF were supplied by Beyotime biotechnology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Anti-EGFR antibody was provided by Shanghai Abcame Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Goat Anti-rabbit IgG/FITC was purchased from Bioss Biotechnology Co., Ltd. (Beijing, China). Goat serum was purchased from Shanxi cell biotech Co., Ltd. (Taiyuan, China). The reagents used in the experiments were analytical grade. All the experiments were performed by using ultrapure water (≥18 MΩ⸱cm).

The GCDs was fabricated by Zhuochi DGG automatic programmable oven (Hangzhou, China). UV-Vis absorption spectra were obtained by the Mapada 760CRT spectrophotometer (Shanghai, China). Fluorescence spectra were performed on the Varian Cary Eclipse fluorescence spectrophotometer (Palo Alto, CA, America). Transmission electron microscopic (TEM) images were obtained by FEI Tecnai G2 20-S-TWIN microscope (Hillsboro, OR, America). The fourier transform infrared spectra (FTIR) was determined with Varian FTIR-640 spectrometer (Palo Alto, CA, America). The XRD spectrum was analyzed by Bruker D8 Advance X-ray diffractometer (Karlsruhe, Germany). Thermo Scientific ESCALAB 250Xi X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (Waltham, MA, America) was used to measure the XPS. Olympus FV-3000 laser confocal microscope (Tokyo, Japan) and BD FACS Calibur flow cytometry (NewYork, NJ, America) was used to explore the uptake of drugs by cells. Drug distribution in vivo was assessed by Bruker small animal optical imager system (Karlsruhe, Germany).

To preparate a kind of CDs with excellent biosafety and easy modification, we adopted p-aminosalicylic acid and p-aminobenzamide as raw materials to synthesize GCDs by one-step hydrothermal method. Because they bear abundant functional groups, and they have been widely applied in various fields including biomedicine [45–47]. Briefly, 135.1 mg p-aminosalicylic acid and 136.2 mg p-aminobenzamide were dissolved in 25 mL ultra-pure water. Then, the solution was transferred into a 50 mL Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 200 oC for 6 h. After the reaction, the solution was centrifuged (10,000 rpm, 10 min), and the supernatant was collected and purified with a 0.22 µm water-based filter membrane. The filtered solution was placed in a dialysis bag (MWCO = 1000 D) within deionized water for 24 h, followed by vacuum freeze-drying to obtain brown powder GCDs. Finally, they were stored in a desiccator for further utilization.

Cisplatin prodrug was prepared according to protocol reported [48]. As shown in Scheme S1, cisplatin (300 mg) was suspended in water (7.5 mL) with heating. When the temperature up to 50 °C, 10.5 mL 30 % hydrogen peroxide was added slowly to react with cisplatin for 2 h, then the mixture was cooled and kept at 0 °C overnight. The light yellow crystals were collected by suction filtration and washed several times with cold water and cold ether, and then it was freeze-dried to obtain the product c,t,c-[PtCl2(OH)2(NH3)2].

Subsequently, c,t,c-[PtCl2(OH)2(NH3)2] (120.0 mg) and succinic anhydride (72.0 mg) were mixed, stirred at room temperature for 24 h. The reaction solution was freeze-dried, and the solid was washed with cold acetone and ether to obtain cisplatin prodrug Pt(IV). Then it was characterized by FTIR.

The GCDs were prepared into a 2.0 mg/mL water solution. 1.0 mg recombinant human epidermal growth factor (EGF) was dissolved in 1.0 mL of PBS (pH 7.4) to prepare 1.0 mg/mL stock solution. 10.0 mg Ce6 DMSO solution, 20.0 mg EDC·HCl, and 20.0 mg NHS water solution were put together and reacted for 30 min at room temperature under magnetic stirring in dark. Then, 500 μL of EGF solution and 4.0 mL of GCDs solution were added, and reacted for 12 h at 4 ℃ in the dark. When the reaction completed, the solution was collected and dialyzed in deionized water with a dialysis bag (MWCO = 3500 D) for 24 h to remove EDC, NHS and any residual, non-cross-linked GCDs, Ce6 and EGF. Lastly, dialysis solution was freeze-dried to obtain GCDs-Ce6-EGF freeze-dried powder.

Pt(IV) prodrug (5.0 mg), EDC·HCl (10.0 mg) water solution and NHS (10.0 mg) water solution were added into a round-bottomed flask and stirred at room temperature for half an hour, then 5.0 mg GCDs-Ce6-EGF was added for another 12 h at 4 ℃ in the dark. The reaction solution was collected and dialyzed with a MWCO 3.5 kD dialysis bag to remove EDC, NHS, unreacted GCDs-Ce6-EGF and Pt(IV). Lastly, dialysis solution was freeze-dried to obtain GCDs-Ce6/Pt-EGF freeze-dried powder.

To calculate the fluorescence quantum yield of GCDs, Rhodamine 6 G (QY=98 %, absolute ethanol as solvent) was adopted as the standard material. The absorbance and the integrated area of the emission spectrum of Rhodamine 6 G and GCDs were saved as the abscissa and ordinate, respectively. Then we used the following formula to calculate the quantum yield:
Qx = Qst (Kx/Kst) (Ƞx/Ƞst)2
of which Qx was the quantum yield of GCDs; Qst was the quantum yield of rhodamine 6 G; Kx was the fitting slope of GCDs; Kst was the fitting slope of Rhodamine 6 G; and Ƞ was the refractive index of the solvent. In this system, Ƞx/Ƞst ≈ 1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Drug loading rate of Ce6 and Pt(IV)
ce:section.ce:para: Drug loading rate is critical for nanomedicine to exert ideal therapeutic effect. Here, drug loading rate of Ce6 and Pt(IV) were performed to evaluate preliminaryly the properties of nanodrug. Firstly, a series of Ce6 standard solutions with a known concentration (0, 0.2, 0.4, 0.6, 0.8 and 1.0 mg/mL) was prepared, and a standard curve was drawn according to absorbance obtained. With the same conditions, the absorption of GCDs-Ce6/Pt-EGF at 650 nm was measured, and the loading of Ce6 in the complex was calculated according to the standard curve.

The loading rate of Pt(IV) was detected by o-phenylenediamine (OPDA) method [49,50]. Firstly, the cisplatin standard solution with a concentration gradient of 0, 5, 10, 20, 30, and 40 μg/mL was mixed with the 1.2 mg/mL OPDA solution in equal volumes at 100 ℃ for 10 min. Then the absorption was measured at 703 nm to obtain the standard curve. The GCDs-Ce6/Pt-EGF was lysed with 4 % SDS or β-Mercaptoethanol (GCDs-Ce6/Pt-EGF: SDS or β-Mercaptoethanol=1:1) to get a clarified liquid. Finally, the obtained liquid was mixed with an equal volume of 1.2 mg/mL OPDA solution to react in an oil bath at 100 ℃ for 10 min, and the absorbance of the reaction solution at 703 nm was measured. The content of cisplatin in the complex was calculated from the standard curve, and then the drug loading rate of cisplatin prodrug was calculated.

The stability studies of the nano assembly are important to justify the product quality. Firstly, GCDs-Ce6/Pt-EGF was dissolved in different solutions (PBS, RPMI 1640 and 10 % FBS) to obtain 1 mg/mL NPs stock solution, and filtered through a 0.22 µm cellulose acetate filter. Then its particle size was measured at a fixed time every day during 7 days.

The freeze-thaw stability was also studied. The NPs stock solution (PBS as the solvent) was frozen in a freezer at − 20 °C for 48 h and then thawed at room temperature for one-hour. The cycles were repeated 2 times. After theses cycles, the sizes of samples were analyzed.

Drug-specific release is critical for mitigating drug side effects. In the presence of GSH, the Pt(IV) prodrug would be reduced to Pt(II) through a chloride bridge (Pt-Cl-S) mediated two electron transfer process [51] . So the Pt(IV) prodrug could be released from GCDs-Ce6/Pt-EGF in the tumor microenvironment where level of GSH was higher than normal tissue.

Then GSH-stimulated drug release was assessed with the o-phenylenediamine method (OPDA) [52,53]. GCDs-Ce6/Pt-EGF was dissolved in 2.0 mL PBS (pH 6.5, containing 10 mM GSH) and dialyzed (MWCO 3.5 kD) in a beaker containing 50 mL pH 5.5 PBS (containing 10 mM GSH). Absorbance obtained at 703 nm was used to determine the amount of cisplatin in the dialysate. An equal GCDs-Ce6/Pt-EGF dissolved in the same volume of PBS (pH 7.4) but without GSH was recruited as control.

Moreover, membrane-permeant NDA was used to detected intracellular depletion of GSH [54], and the human esophageal squamous cell carcinoma (KYSE-150) was selected as the experimental model cell. The cells in the logarithmic growth phase were seeded in dishes. After 24 h, 1.0 mL Pt(IV) and GCDs-Ce6/Pt-EGF (serum-free medium as the solution, Pt(IV) concentration from 0 to 12 μg/mL) were added respectively. Another 24 h later the cells were imaged.

To assess the PDT potential of nano-assambly, the photodynamic properties of GCDs-Ce6/Pt-EGF in vitro were characterized by commercial probes DPBF and DCFH-DA. Briefly, 10 μL of GCDs-Ce6/Pt-EGF (1 mg/mL) was mixed with DPBF (60 µM) and DCFH-DA (20 µM), respectively. Then the mixture was irradiated with 660 nm laser (0.5 W/cm2) for different times (0, 1, 2, 3, 4 and 5 min). Meanwhile, the absorption spectra and fluorescence spectroscopy of solution were measured after irradiation, and PBS, mixed with DPBF (60 µM) or DCFH-DA (20 µM) and irradiated with 660 nm laser (0.5 W/cm2) for different times, was used as blank control.

The uptaken of drugs by cells is the basic condition for the efficacy. Therefore, the ability of the drug uptaken was studied firstly before treatment. The KYSE-150 cells were seeded in confocal dishes at a density of 1 × 105 cells/well and cultured. After 24 h, 2.0 mL of GCDs-Ce6/Pt-EGF (serum-free medium as the solution, Ce6 5.0 μg/mL) was added to each dish and incubated for 0.5, 1, 2, and 4 h. After rinsed with PBS, confocal imaging was performed via three channels, bright field, green channel (Ex: 488 nm, Em: 490–560 nm) and red channel (Ex: 630 nm, Em: 650–750 nm). The quantitative analysis of the imaging result was implemented by ImageJ software, and datas were represented as means ± SD of three independent experiments, and P < 0.05 was termed statistically significant. At the same time, flow cytometry was used to determine the uptaken of GCDs-Ce6/Pt-EGF by cells. Briefly, KYSE-150 cells were seeded into a 6-well plate at a density of 1 × 105 cells/well. When the cell density reached about 80 %, 2.0 mL GCDs-Ce6/Pt-EGF (serum-free medium as the solution, Ce6 5.0 μg/mL) was added to each well. After incubated for 0.5, 1, 2, and 4 h, the cells were collected and subjected to flow cytometry assay.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Targeting ability and specificity of GCDs-Ce6/Pt-EGF
ce:section.ce:para: Targeted therapy can overcome serious side effects of off-target drugs, to determine the targeting ability of GCDs-Ce6/Pt-EGF to cells with EGFR expression, KYSE-150 cells were seeded into 6-well plate at a density of 1 × 105 cells/well. When the cell confluence was about 80 %, 2.0 mL GCDs (60 μg/mL), Ce6 (5.0 μg/mL), GCDs-Ce6-EGF (Ce6 concentration 5.0 μg/mL) and GCDs-Ce6/Pt-EGF (Ce6 concentration 5.0 μg/mL) were administered respectively. After another 4 h, cell imaging was performed with the same settings as mentioned above.

To determine the targeting specificity of GCDs-Ce6/Pt-EGF, cells with different expression level of EGFR, KYSE-150, Human breast cells (MCF-7) and Human normal esophageal epithelial cells (HEEC) were recruited and subjected to western blot, immunofluorescence imaging and cell imaging. Firstly, expression of EGFR in cell lines mentioned above was detected by western blot and immunofluorescence imaging. Secondly, the uptake capacity of different cells to drug were confirmed with CLSM. Moreover, EGF, as the competitive reagent of GCDs-Ce6/Pt-EGF (Ce6 concentration 5.0 μg/mL), was administered to KYSE-150 cells to verify the effect on drug uptake. The quantitative analysis of the imaging result was implemented by ImageJ software, and datas were represented as means ± SD of three independent experiments, and P < 0.05 was termed statistically significant.

Tumor metastasis is the main reason for recurrence after treatment, so it is of great significance for drugs to inhibit cell migration. The effect of drugs on the migration ability of cancer cell was assessed by the wound healing assay. The KYSE-150 cells were seeded in a 6-well plate (1 ×105 cells/well). When the cell confluence was about 80 %, vertical scratches was made with a tip. Then 2.0 mL GCDs (60 μg /mL), Ce6 (5.0 μg/mL), Cisplatin (11.35 μg/mL), GCDs-Ce6-EGF (Ce6 5.0 μg/mL) and GCDs-Ce6/Pt-EGF (Ce6 5.0 μg/mL) were added and incubated for 0, 12, and 24 h, respectively. The width of wound was recorded by ImageJ software. The datas were represented as means±SD of three independent experiments, and P < 0.05 was termed statistically significant.

In order to test the safety and therapeutic effect of nanomedicine at the cellular level, the phototoxicity and dark-toxicity of drugs were evaluated by MTT assay. KYSE-150 cells, in the logarithmic growth phase, were seeded in a 96-well plate and cultured for 24 h. GCDs, Ce6, Cisplatin, and GCDs-Ce6-EGF and GCDs-Ce6/Pt-EGF diluted into different concentrations with RPMI 1640 without serum were administered to the cells and incubated for 24 h. Then MTT assay was performed according the protocol of operation manual. Briefly, 100 μL MTT reagent (0.5 mg/mL) was added to the each well and 4 h later DMSO, as the solvent, was added. The absorbance at 490 nm was detected by a microplate reader to evaluate their cytotoxicity. The cell survival rate was determined by the following formula:
survival rate = (ODtreatment group-ODblank)/(ODcontrol group-ODblank) ×100 %·

To image the cell viability after treatment, KYSE-150 cells were inoculated in a 6-well plate. GCDs, Ce6, Cisplatin, GCDs-Ce6-EGF and GCDs-Ce6/Pt-EGF were administered when the cell confluence was about 80 %. After incubating for 24 h, the cells were subjected to 660 nm laser irradiation (0.5 W/cm2, 5 min). Then, Calcein-AM/PI probe was added and cell fluorescent imaging was taken.

To quantify the cell death induced by treatment, Annexin V-FITC/PI apoptosis test kit was recruited. KYSE-150 cells were inoculated in a 6-well plate. After 24 h, GCDs, Ce6, Cisplatin, GCDs-Ce6-EGF and GCDs-Ce6/Pt-EGF (the same concentration as above) were added. After another 24 h, the cells were subjected to 660 nm laser irradiation (0.5 W/cm2, 5 min). Then the cells were collected, stained with Annexin V-FITC/PI for 30 min in dark and subjected to flow cytometry analysis.

To examine the photodynamic effects of nanomedicines at the cellular level, ROS generated after treatment with GCDs-Ce6/Pt-EGF was assessed by DCFH-DA, a commercial ROS detection probe. The KYSE-150 cells were seeded in a 6-well plate with 1 × 105 cells/well. After 24 h, GCDs (60 μg/mL), Ce6 (5.0 μg/mL), GCDs-Ce6-EGF (Ce6 5.0 μg/mL), GCDs-Ce6/Pt-EGF (Ce6 5.0 μg/mL) in 2.0 mL serum-free medium were added to each well and incubated for 24 h. Then medium was removed and 1.0 mL of PBS containing DCFH-DA (10 μM) was added, and the cells were irradiated with 660 nm laser (0.5 W/cm2, 5 min). After 20 min, DCFH-DA was removed and washed with PBS, and images were collected using a fluorescence microscope.

In order to study the effect of nano-drugs in vivo, tumor-bearing balb/c nude mice were selected as models. The mice were purchased from Beijing Vital River Laboratory Animal Technology Co., Ltd. All the experiments on mice were performed strictly in accordance with the Institutional Animal Care and Use Committee of Shanxi Medical University. The mice were raised in a sterile 12:12-h light-dark photoperiod environment, and food and water were all autoclaved. When the mice were about 6 weeks old, 1 × 107 KYSE-150 cells in 1640 medium were implanted in the left armpit of mouse by subcutaneous injection after anesthetized with isoflurane. The length (a) and width (b) of the tumor were measured with a vernier caliper every 3 days and the volume of tumor was calculated according to the formula V = a×b2/2. The drug intervention was started when the volume of tumor was about 180 mm3. Mice after treatments with 15 % body weight loss or tumor diameter longer than 1.5 cm would be euthanized according to the standard animal protocol.

To determine the biodistribution of drugs, 200 μL of Ce6 and GCDs-Ce6/Pt-EGF (Ce6 2.0 mg/kg per mouse) dissolved in PBS were injected via tail vein. Using the multimodal Xtreme imaging system, the circulation process of the two drugs in mice was monitored, and fluorescence imaging (Ex/Em: 630/700 nm) was obtained at different time nodes (2, 4, 6, 8, 10, 12 and 24 h). The mice were sacrificed 24 h after drugs administration, and tissues and organs (heart, liver, spleen, lung, kidney, and tumor) were dissected for fluorescence imaging at the same excitation wavelength.

To explore the therapeutic advantages of nanomedicine, changes in tumor volume and biosafety indicators of different treatments were studied. When the tumor volume was about 180 mm3, the nude mice were randomly divided into 9 groups (5 mice/group) based on the purpose of treatment: (1) PBS + L; (2) Ce6; (3) GCDs-Ce6-EGF; (4) Cisplatin; (5) Cisplatin + L; (6) GCDs-Ce6/Pt-EGF; (7) Ce6 + L; (8) GCDs-Ce6-EGF + L; (9) GCDs-Ce6/Pt-EGF + L. Various drugs (final dose of Ce6 2.0 mg/kg, Cisplatin 4.54 mg/kg) dissolved in 200 uL PBS were administrated via tail vein injection on day 0 and day 3. 10 h after drugs administration, 660 nm laser irradiation was performed (0.5 W/cm2, 5 min). The mice were sacrificed on 21th day, and during this period the health status of the mice was monitored. The tumor volume and the weight of the mice were measured and recorded every 3 days. Pictures of mice were taken on day 0, 7, 14, 21. Dissected tumors were subjected to imaging and weighting when the experimental procedure was completed. The datas were represented as means ± SD of five independent experiments, and P < 0.05 was termed statistically significant.

To further compare the therapeutic effect of different therapy groups, the Ki67 and terminal deoxynucleotidyl transferase-mediated dUTP nick-end labeling (TUNEL) staining in tumor tissues was performed.

To assess the systematic impact of drug administration on mice, the serum levels of liver function markers aspartate aminotransferase (AST), alanine aminotransferase (ALT), and kidney function markers urea nitrogen (BUN) of mice were measured. Blood samples were collected from mice orbital sinus and centrifuged at 3000 rpm for 10 min, and then the upper layer serum was collected to test. AST, ALT, and BUN were determined with specific commercial assay kit.

After the blood collection was completed, the mice were euthanasia. Organs and tumor tissues were dissected and fixed with 4 % paraformaldehyde. Hematoxylin-eosin (H&E) staining was performed and imaged under microscope.

In this work, GCDs played an important role in the imaging, whereas fluorescence properties of the carbon dots could be affected by ratio of raw materials, reaction temperature and during. So, we firstly optimized the condition to fabricate GCDs. It was proved that p-aminosalicylic acid and p-aminobenzamide with molar ratio 1:1 (dissolved in ultrapure water) reacted at 200 ℃ for 6 h rendered best fluorescence properties (Fig. S1). After getting GCDs, we characterized their physiochemical properties. GCDs were uniformly dispersed quasi-sphere with average size 4.72 nm assessed with TEM and AFM ( Fig. 1A-B). As shown in Fig. S2, there was a strong diffraction peak at 2θ= 25° in XRD of GCDs, indicating that GCDs were mainly composed of amorphous carbon.

The surface functional groups of GCDs were determined with FTIR and XPS. As found via FTIR, there were peaks at 3430 cm-1, 1623 cm-1, 1405 cm-1, 1114 cm-1, and 619 cm-1 which were attributed to the stretching vibration of O-H/N-H, CC/CO, C-N, and bending vibration of C-O and N-H, respectively (Fig. 1C) [55,56]. To further confirm the elements and chemical bonds in GCDs, XPS analysis was performed. It displayed three prominent peaks at 287.5 eV, 403.8 eV, and 535.1 eV which corresponded to characteristic peaks of C1s, N1s and O1s, respectively (Fig. 1D). Under high-resolution spectrum, peaks at 284.7 eV, 285.6 eV, 287.2 eV and 289.1 eV were observed, which corresponded to CC/C-C, C-N, C-O, CO respectively [57], and peaks at 399 eV and 400.6 eV corresponded to C-N-C and N-H respectively [58]. Whereas peaks at 531.3 eV and 532.8 eV corresponded to CO and C-OH/C-O-C [59] respectively (Fig. 1E-G). The XPS results were consistent with that of FTIR. Moreover, optical property of GCDs was validated. GCDs had an absorption peak at 490 nm, which could be attributed to n→π * electronic transitions between sp2 carbons and the functional groups with lone-pair electrons (e.g., CO) of GCDs [60,61]. And the largest emission wavelength was at 530 nm (Fig. 1H), which was consistent with the result of Fig. S3. With Rhodamine 6 G as the standard reference material, the fluorescence quantum yield of GCDs was calculated to be 10.62 % (Table S1), which was higher many reported work [62,63]. Meanwhile, the optical stability of GCDs was explored and it displayed excellent stability in various complicated environments (Fig. S4). The above results demonstrated that GCDs bore amino and carboxyl groups which were able to be functionalized. GCDs were also endowed with excellent optical stability which is essential to be developed into bioimaging agent.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and characterization of nano-assembly drug
ce:section.ce:para: Given GCDs were rich in functional groups such as amino and carboxyl, it could be modified as a drug carrier. Then photosensitizer Ce6 and tumor targeting unit EGF were grafted onto GCDs by chemical bonding to get GCDs-Ce6-EGF. To overcome the side-effect of free cisplatin and MDR inducing by tumor environment, prodrug Pt(IV), one cisplatin derivate, was obtained by hydroxylation and carboxylation, and characterized with FTIR. We observed two novel bands corresponding to O-H and Pt-O stretches at 3515 cm-1 and 557 cm-1 besides that of cisplatin which were weakened due to the depletion of OH during the oxidation to produce Pt(IV) (Fig. S5), and similar to that observed by other authors [64]. Then Pt(IV) was covalently attached to GCDs-Ce6-EGF and the target product GCDs-Ce6/Pt-EGF was obtained.

After getting the nano-assembly drug, GCDs-Ce6/Pt-EGF, its physiochemical properties were determined. Firstly, the morphology and particle diameter were assessed by TEM. It was found that GCDs-Ce6/Pt-EGF was spherical with an average diameter about 80–100 nm ( Fig. 2A), which was best for tumor site enrichment by the enhanced permeability and retention (EPR) effect [65,66]. The dynamic light scattering (DLS) further proved that nano-assembly had an average hydrodynamic particle size of 90.56 ± 2.07 nm with a relatively low polydispersity index (PDI: 0.161) (Fig. 2B), which indicated prepared nano-assembly highly monodispersed [67]. XPS results, showed that there were characteristic Pt4f peaks were attributed to Pt4+ (Fig. S6 and Fig. 2C) [68], indicating successfully loading of Pt(IV) on nano-assembly [69]. Zeta potential result showed that GCDs were positively charged, and GCDs-Ce6-EGF or GCDs-Ce6/Pt-EGF were negatively charged (Fig. 2D), which partially proved the drug successfully prepared. The typical peaks of GCDs and Ce6 presented in the UV-Vis spectra (Fig. 2E), fluorescence spectra (Fig. S7) and FTIR (Fig. S8) of GCDs-Ce6/Pt-EGF, proving nano-assembly was successfully synthesized and there was not a quenching effect of Ce6 in the GCDs cluster. So, it could be used as a fluorescent imaging agent for imaging-guided therapy. In addition, in the nanosystem, the drug loading rate of Ce6 and Pt (IV) was 8.53 % and 19.36 % respectively (Fig. S9), which were higher than some reported research [70,71]. This is important for achieving a high therapeutic effect. The stability of nanomaterials is critical for its biomedical application. So the particle size variation of nanocomposites in different environments and its freeze-thaw (FT) stability were investigated to assess its stability. It was proved that the as developed nanosystem displayed good stability in PBS, RPMI 1640 and 10 % FBS as long as 7 days (Fig. S10). As shown in Fig. S11, after 1, 2 times freeze-thaw cycles, the sizes of nano-assembly had no obvious change, indicating this nano-assembly had excellent stability.

To assess the stimulated drug release of nano-assembly, GCDs-Ce6/Pt-EGF was added into GSH-containing (10 mM) PBS (pH 6.5, corresponding the microenvironment of cancer cells). Cisplatin was found to be released and after 24 h it tended to be balanced with 60 % release rate. In contrast, in PBS (pH 7.4) without GSH, the release rate was only about 20 %. And it showed almost no obvious release from GCDs-Ce6/Pt-EGF at pH 6.5 conditions compared to that in pH 7.4 (Fig. 2F), indicating GSH was a major factor in drug release. Furthermore, to assess the depletion of GSH by nano-assembly drug, one commercial GSH probe, NDA, was applied to the cells incubated with Pt (IV) and GCDs-Ce6/Pt-EGF. It showed a concentration-dependent decrease of fluorescence with adding of drugs (Fig. S12), suggesting the consuming of GSH. The above results indicated that GCDs-Ce6/Pt-EGF could be released specifically in tumor tissue with high level of GSH, avoiding damage to normal cells. Whereas Ce6 still had excellent photodynamic property with irradiation even if it was loading on the GCDs-Ce6/Pt-EGF.

To explore the photodynamic properties of GCDs-Ce6/Pt-EGF in vitro, DPBF and DCFH-DA, widely utilized ROS probe, were adopted. The absorption peak of DPBF would decrease when it is exposed to ROS-rich environment. Whereas, when DCFH-DA is activated to DCF, the fluorescence intensity of activated DCF would increase under the same conditions. It can be seen from the Fig. 2G, under the irradiation of 660 nm laser, the fluorescence intensity of DCF ascended, indicating continuous ROS generation. Similarly, with the increase of irradiation during from 0 m to 5 min, the absorption peak of DPBF at 410 nm continually decreased (Fig. 2H), indicating singlet oxygen was produced under laser irradiation. Whereas, it had no obvious change in the control group (Fig. S13). Thus, it was proved that the GCDs-Ce6/Pt-EGF was endowed with excellent photodynamic performance, which laid the foundation for the realization of photodynamic therapy in vitro and in vivo.

On the basis of excellent in vitro properties, the therapeutic effect of the drug at the biological level is more important. The uptaken efficacy of drugs by cells is a prerequisite for its therapeutic effect. To assess the uptaken of GCDs-Ce6/Pt-EGF by tumor cells, KYSE-150, one esophageal tumor cell line, was recruited. The cells incubated with GCDs-Ce6/Pt-EGF showed fluorescent signal derived from GCDs (green) and Ce6 (red) was observed with confocal laser scanning microscope (CLSM). The intensity of fluorescent signal increased with the extension of incubating time ( Fig. 3A-B). Moreover, to further confirm the uptaken of GCDs-Ce6/Pt-EGF by tumor cells, flow cytometry was adopted to analyze the fluorescent signal derived from Ce6. It showed that the fluorescence intensity augmented with the incubation time from 0 to 4 h (Fig. 3C), which was consistent with that of CLSM. Those results suggested that GCDs-Ce6/Pt-EGF could be easily uptaken by tumor cells, which ensured its therapeutic effect.

ce:section.ce:section-title: EGF mediated tumor cell targeting of GCDs-Ce6/Pt-EGF
ce:section.ce:para: Active targeted delivery of drugs, achieving generally via cell surface receptors, is an effective way to overcome off-target toxicity in clinical [72]. Most of the esophageal tumor cells express EGFR, which could be used as the target for drug binding. To assess the targeting ability of drugs with/without EGF, we compared internalization efficiency of GCDs, Ce6, GCDs-Ce6-EGF, and GCDs-Ce6/Pt-EGF under CLSM. It showed that, although GCDs, Ce6 could be uptaken by KYSE-150, GCDs-Ce6-EGF and GCDs-Ce6/Pt-EGF administration rendered much stronger fluorescent signal ( Fig. 4A-B), indicating that EGF facilitated the uptaken of nano-assembly drug. To further clarify the specificity of EGF mediated cell targeting, KYSE-150 cells were pretreated with EGF before GCDs-Ce6/Pt-EGF administration. It showed that EGF pretreatment significantly reduced GCDs or Ce6 fluorescent signal (Fig. 4C-D), indicating compromised nano-assembly drug was uptaken due to EGF competitively binding EGFR of KYSE-150 cells. Above results suggested that EGF of nano-assembly drug enhanced uptake and cell targeting capacity of GCDs-Ce6/Pt-EGF by EGFR bearing tumor cells.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Expression of EGFR affect the uptake of GCDs-Ce6/Pt-EGF
ce:section.ce:para: GCDs-Ce6/Pt-EGF or GCDs-Ce6-EGF was uptaken more than free drugs, but the molecular-level uptake mechanism could be studied. To determine how the expression of EGFR of cell affect nano-assembly drug uptake, normal esophageal epithelial cells (HEEC), esophageal tumor cells (KYSE-150), and breast tumor cells (MCF-7) were recruited. The expression level EGFR in HEEC, KYSE-150, and MCF-7 cells were assessed by immunofluorescent staining. It showed KYSE-150 got higher EGFR expression than HEEC and MCF-7 ( Fig. 5A-B). Moreover, The expression of EGFR in above mentioned three cell lines was further validated by western blot (Fig. S14). It was showed that KYSE-150 got higher EGFR expression than HEEC and MCF-7, which was consistent with the result of immunofluorescent staining. After validating the expression of EGFR in different cell lines, we proceeded to check if expression of EGFR affects the uptake of GCDs-Ce6/Pt-EGF. As showed in Fig. 5C-D, KYSE-150, with highest EGFR expression, displayed strongest fluorescent signal derived from GCDs and Ce6 among the above three cell lines, suggesting that EGF prominently facilitated the uptaken of GCDs-Ce6/Pt-EGF by EGFR bearing cells.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro tumor cell killing capacity of nano-assembly drug
ce:section.ce:para: After validating its physiochemical property and targeting ability, we proceeded to check the tumor cell killing capacity of nano-assambly drug via wound healing, MTT, apoptosis assays and Calcein-AM/PI assay. KYSE-150 cells were treated with GCDs, Ce6, GCDs-Ce6-EGF, Cisplatin, and GCDs-Ce6/Pt-EGF. As showed in Fig. S15, incubating with GCDs, Ce6, GCDs-Ce6-EGF did not obviously alter the scratch width compared with PBS treated control group. Cisplatin and GCDs-Ce6/Pt-EGF administration significantly inhibited the healing of scratched wound. Cisplatin had been proved to inhibit the migration, and promote the apoptosis of cancer cells [73,74]. We found that GCDs-Ce6/Pt-EGF rendered even better effect than that of Cisplatin. It indicated that nano-assembly drug could restrict the tumor cell migration, meanwhile, EGF facilitated tumor cell targeting and enhanced its effect comparing with Cisplatin.

To further confirm the cell killing ability of nano-assembly drug, MTT assay ( Fig. 6A) was recruited in KYSE-150 cells treated with Ce6, Cisplatin, GCDs-Ce6-EGF and GCDs-Ce6/Pt-EGF for 24 h. It showed that Ce6 and GCDs-Ce6-EGF administration did not induce obvious cell death even in 5.0 μg/mL if without 660 nm laser irradiation. But with laser irradiation, Ce6 and GCDs-Ce6-EGF displayed enhanced cell killing ability in a dose-dependent manner, of which GCDs-Ce6-EGF showed better effect than Ce6. Cisplatin itself induced cell death in a dose-dependent manner, but laser irradiation did not produce synergistic effect. In the various treatments, GCDs-Ce6/Pt-EGF rendered most prominent cell killing capacity, and laser irradiation aggravated the cell killing effect. It could be attributed to the synergistic effect of EGF mediated targeting, cisplatin induced cytotoxicity, and Ce6 induced photodynamic effect.

Based on the results of MTT, flow cytometry was applied to quantify the drug induced cell death labeled with Annexin V-FITC/PI. KYSE-150 cells were subjected to GCDs, Ce6, Cisplatin, GCDs-Ce6-EGF, GCDs-Ce6/Pt-EGF together with laser irradiation, and cell apoptosis rate induced was 2.63 %, 35.45 %, 24.28 %, 38.34 %, and 66.93 %, respectively (Fig. 6B). GCDs-Ce6/Pt-EGF treatment led to the highest cells apoptosis rate, whereas PBS together with laser irradiation only led to 1.17 % cell apoptosis, suggesting that irradiation itself had neglectable cell killing effect. To visualize the cell apoptosis induced by drug treatment, KYSE-150 cells subjected to GCDs, Ce6, GCDs-Ce6-EGF, Cisplatin, GCDs-Ce6/Pt-EGF were stained with Calcein-AM/PI probe and imaged. As showed in Fig. 6C, without or with laser irradiation, PBS and GCDs did not induce visible cell apoptosis. Ce6 and GCDs-Ce6-EGF treatment also did not lead to obvious cell death, but together with laser irradiation it rendered increased cell death as stained by PI, of which GCDs-Ce6-EGF induced more cell death than Ce6 only. Without laser irradiation, cisplatin and GCDs-Ce6/Pt-EGF led to most prominent cell death compared with other treatment. Laser irradiation substantially enhanced GCDs-Ce6/Pt-EGF treatment induced cell death whereas not affect that of cisplatin. This result was consistent with those derived from other apoptosis assays.

Photosensitizer Ce6 after irradiation induces cell death by generating ROS. To assess the ROS level in above drugs treated KYSE-150 cells, commercial ROS fluorescence indicator (DCFH-DA) was recruited. In the tumor cell, DCFH-DA would turn into DCF by intracellular esterase, the green fluorescence intensity of activated DCF would increase with the generation of ROS. As showed in Fig. S16, in Ce6, GCDs-Ce6-EGF and GCDs-Ce6/Pt-EGF treated cells strong green fluorescence signal presented, whereas no visible signal was detected in PBS, GCDs, and Cisplatin treated cells, which could be attributed to the excellent PDT performance of Ce6.

While the drug can inhibit tumor cells, it cannot produce toxic side effects on normal cells at the same time, to verify the cytotoxicity of GCDs-Ce6/Pt-EGF on normal esophageal cells, HEEC was subjected to GCDs-Ce6/Pt-EGF as high as 18.0 μg/mL of Pt(IV) for 24 h. As showed in Fig. S17, it did not induce obvious cells death, indicating GCDs-Ce6/Pt-EGF had neglectable cytotoxicity to normal esophageal cells. It could be attributed to three aspects. Firstly, GCDs-Ce6/Pt-EGF had active targeting capability, which avoided the off-target toxicity; secondly, the toxic platinum was only released in the GSH-rich tumor microenvironment; Thirdly, Ce6 only exerted photodynamic therapy effect with laser irradiation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Targeted fluorescence imaging and anti-tumor effect in vivo
ce:section.ce:para: The distribution of drug in tumors would influence its therapeutic effect. To assess the distribution of nano-assembly drug, Ce6 and GCDs-Ce6/Pt-EGF (Ce6 2.0 mg/kg) were injected into mice via tail vein of KYSE-150 tumor-bearing mice. Fluorescence imaging was taken via multimodal Xtreme imaging system at different time points. As showed in upper panel of Fig. 7A-B, Ce6 was dispersed throughout the body including tumor 2 h post-injection, and maintained at high level till 8 h after injection, and then decreased subsequently. Noteworthy, 24 h after Ce6 injection, fluorescent signal was still visible in the tumor partially due to excessive vascularization of tumor. In comparison, in the GCDs-Ce6/Pt-EGF administration mice, tumor had stronger signal than that of free Ce6 injected mice, which maintained till 24 h post-injection (Fig. 7A-B), indicating the tumor targeting and retention effect of nano-assembly drug, which could be attributed to the detoxification and metabolic function of liver and kidney [75]. As observed, the fluorescence signal in tumor reached the strongest intensity 10 h post-injection, indicating that the ideal time window for PDT should be the same time point.

After determining the spatiotemporal distribution of drug in mice, we performed drug intervention in the tumor-bearing mice as illustrated in Fig. 7C, and pictures of mice were taken every 7 days till the end of treatment (Fig. 7D). It could be seen that PBS, Ce6, and GCDs-Ce6-EGF administration did not affect the growth of tumor obviously. Cisplatin and GCDs-Ce6/Pt-EGF administration inhibited the growth of tumor moderately, of which GCDs-Ce6/Pt-EGF exhibited better effect. With laser irradiation, Ce6, GCDs-Ce6-EGF, and GCDs-Ce6/Pt-EGF displayed enhanced tumor inhibiting effect, compared with drugs only. For cisplatin, laser irradiation did not alter its tumor inhibiting effect. Of all the intervention, GCDs-Ce6/Pt-EGF with laser rendered the most prominent tumor inhibiting effect. The dissected tumor volume (Fig. 7E) and weight (Fig. 7F) measurement results showed the same trend. The treatment, except cisplatin and cisplatin + L, did not affect the weight of mice (Fig. 7G). Cisplatin treatment caused weight loss could be attributed to its side effect of chemotherapy. The above results suggest that synergistic effect of chemotherapy and photodynamic therapy derived from GCDs-Ce6/Pt-EGF, which was much better than either of single mode treatment. To further confirm the enhanced therapeutic effect of GCDs-Ce6/Pt-EGF, slices of tumor tissues were subjected to Ki67 staining and TUNEL staining. As shown in Fig. 7H, the fewer Ki67 positive signals and more TUNEL positive signals were observed in the GCDs-Ce6/Pt-EGF group, indicating the inhibitory effects on tumor proliferation.

To evaluate the biosafety of drugs, the serum level of AST, ALT and BUN, which reflected the function of liver and kidney, was assessed respectively. As showed in Fig. 8A, of all drugs treatments, only cisplatin and the cisplatin + L led to increased ALT, but they did not alter the AST and BUN level. It suggested that cisplatin, as one widely utilized chemotherapy agent, could lead to liver injury in some extend, which was the also reason why we fabricated the nano-assembly drug which successfully avoided the side effect of cisplatin by tumor targeting modification. Moreover, we also did the histochemical staining of the main organs, including heart, liver, spleen, lung, kidney of mice subjected for drug intervention. It was showed that neither of the drugs induce visible alternation in histological structure of those organs (Fig. 8B). Those results suggested that as fabricated nano-assembly drug had excellent biosafety and it could be potentially applied to the treatment of malignant tumors especially with EGFR expression in clinic.

As the second most deadly gastrointestinal cancer, esophageal cancer is threatening the life of people worldwide [1]. Currently, the treatment of esophageal cancer involves a multidisciplinary approach, including surgery, chemotherapy, radiotherapy and so on. But only 10 % of esophageal cancer patients are eligible for direct surgery at the time of diagnosis with early micrometastases [76]. Meanwhile, taxanes, platinum drugs, fluoropyrimidines, and epirubicin are commonly used chemotherapy drugs. However, the limitations of poor selectivity and low solubility in water hinder the clinical application. Radiotherapy is an important local control modality for esophageal cancer treatment, and the poor local control effect of radiotherapy on esophageal cancer patients is still a problem in clinical application [77]. In practice, the underlying causes of cancer persistence and lack of successful treatment are partially attributed to the tumor microenvironment (TME), which contains peripheral blood vessels, immune cells, fibroblasts, signaling molecules, and the extracellular matrix [78]. TME is characterized by hypoxia, acidic pH, higher H2O2, excess GSH, and overexpressed enzymes, which not only promote tumor angiogenesis and metastasis, but also contribute to treatment resistance.

In order to overcome the above therapeutic shortcomings, various multifunctional nanoplatforms have been used to modulate the TME, such as neutralizing acidic pH, overcoming tumor hypoxia, depleting excess GSH, and normalizing the immune microenvironment to further enhance the efficacy of cancer therapy [14,17]. Moreover, they can be designed for drug delivery with improved pharmacokinetic properties to enable active or passive targeting of cancer tissues and reduce unintended toxicity. Meanwhile, these nanoplatforms can enhance drug absorption to specific tumor tissues while controlling drug tissue distribution. But the transition of nanoplatforms to the clinic still faces several challenges. One of the main challenges is that some nanoplatforms with long circulation times may cause drug leakage at unexpected sites. For example, pegylated liposomal doxorubicin is a common chemotherapeutic agent, but it can cause hand-foot syndrome [78]. Another challenge is that some nanocarriers can be rapidly cleared from the bloodstream by the reticuloendothelial defense mechanism [79]. Furthermore, the burst drug release and nonspecific uptake of the mononuclear phagocytic system ultimately hinders the application of nanomedicine in esophageal cancer therapy [80]. In this study, the nano-assembly, GCDs-Ce6/Pt-EGF, overcomed the off-target toxicity by active targeting of cancer tissues. And it was metabolized after 24 h of intravenous injection, avoiding the cumulative toxicity, which could be proved with the biosafety experiment. Moreover, The GSH-responsive drug release behavior of GCDs-Ce6/Pt-EGF occurred only at the tumor site, avoiding the nonspecific uptake and increasing efficacy. The above results showed that GCDs-Ce6/Pt-EGF had great potential for clinical application. Of course, there were some limitations in this work, such as the treatment mechanism of different ways remains to be studied at the molecular level.

Tumor is one of the major threatens to our life. Various therapeutic approaches have been developed, such as surgery, chemotherapy and radiotherapy. Regretfully, the outcomes of those treatments are not satisfactory. In this work, to address the problem of poor efficacy of chemotherapy and PDT therapy due to the lack of targeting and high GSH at the tumor site. An all-in-one targeting theranostic nanoplatform was proposed and constructed，which with the ability to specifically target tumor site, response and release chemotherapeutic agents and reduce GSH levels. In this nanoplatform, GCDs with the excellent optical properties, modifiability and low toxicity were fabricated as a carrier, and then carried the chemotherapeutic drug cisplatin, the photodynamic reagent Ce6 and the targeting ligand EGF. Firstly, this nano-assembly avoided nonspecific uptake of normal healthy tissue via the active targeting of EGF and increased the cisplatin content in the tumor site. Meanwhile, it could respond to the tumor microenvironment, so it only had effective at the tumor site, which prevented the major defect of the pure cisplatin during the drug metabolism process and the loss the effect of chemotherapy and PDT. With the release of actived platinum from GCDs-Ce6/Pt-EGF at the tumor site, the GSH concentration in the tumor site gradually decreased, which alleviated tumor drug resistance and enhanced oxidative stress. Thereby this nano-assembly obtained enhanced chemotherapy and PDT compared with free cisplatin and free Ce6. The in vitro and in vivo imaging results of GCDs-Ce6/Pt-EGF showed it possessed excellent imaging performance and had great potential for imaging-guided precision therapy. Furthermore, the therapeutic effects proved that the combined treatment of enhanced chemotherapy and PDT had the best therapeutic effect with excellent biological safety. In the current situation of inaccurate preoperative diagnosis, lack of intraoperative navigation, incomplete surgical resection, and severe liver and kidney toxicity caused by chemotherapy drugs, the strategies of combining imaging and treatment provided a new idea for treating esophageal cancer.
